Both React Context and Redux solve the same basic problem: getting data from one part of your app to another without passing props through ten layers of components (“prop drilling”). But they solve it very differently under the hood, and that difference is exactly why people get confused about when to use which.
How Context Actually Works
Context is not a state management library. It’s a way to skip levels when
passing data down the component tree. That’s it. React itself doesn’t do
anything smart with the data — it just makes a value available to any
descendant that asks for it.
Here’s the internal mechanism, in simple words:

• createContext() creates a "channel." A <Context.Provider value={...}>
puts a value onto that channel for everything below it in the tree.

• Any component that calls useContext(Context) subscribes to the nearest
Provider above it.

• When the Provider’s value changes (React checks this by reference, using Object.is), React re-renders every single component that consumes that context, no matter how small the change was or which part of the value they actually care about.
That last point is the important one. If your context value is { user, theme, notifications } and only notifications changed, every component reading user or theme from that context re-renders too — because Context has no concept of "I only care about part of this object." It's all-or-nothing. Wrapping a consumer in React.memo doesn't save you either; memoization only blocks re-renders from prop changes, not from context changes, because reading context is treated as reading a dependency, not a prop.
This is why large, frequently-changing global state stuffed into one big Context tends to make apps slow — everything downstream re-renders on every update, and the usual fix people reach for is to split one big context into several small, narrowly-scoped ones (e.g. separate UserContext and ThemeContext instead of one AppContext).
How Redux Actually Works
Redux is a proper state management library, and its internals are built
specifically to avoid the problem above.

• There’s a single store holding the entire app’s state as one object.

• State changes only ever happen by dispatching an action (a plain
object describing what happened) to a reducer (a pure function that
takes the old state + action and returns new state). Nothing else is
allowed to mutate state directly.

• Components read state using useSelector(selectorFn). This is the key
difference from Context: the selector function picks out exactly the slice of state a component needs, and Redux only re-renders that component if the selected value actually changed (checked with a shallow equality check), not whenever anything in the store changes.
So where Context re-renders “everyone downstream, always,” Redux re-renders “only the components whose selected slice actually changed.” That fine-grained subscription model is the whole reason Redux scales better for large, busy, shared state.
Redux also comes with things Context has no opinion on at all:

• Middleware (like redux-thunk or redux-saga) for handling async logic
and side effects in a structured, testable way.

• DevTools with time-travel debugging — you can literally step backward
and forward through every state change that happened in your app.

• A predictable, centralized place to reason about “what can change my state and why,” since every change flows through an action + reducer.
Side by Side
React Context Redux What it is A way to skip prop drilling A full state management system Re-render behavior All consumers re-render on any value change Only components whose selected slice changed re-render Setup Built into React, zero extra code Requires store, actions, reducers (Redux Toolkit makes this much lighter) Async / side effects Nothing built in — you roll your own Middleware ecosystem (thunk, saga, RTK Query) DevTools / time travel None Yes, very mature Best for Data that’s mostly static and read by many components Data that changes often and is shared across many components
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When to Choose What (Simple Rule of Thumb)
Use Context when:

• The data barely changes — theme, logged-in user, locale/language, feature flags, a design-system config.

• Only a handful of components need it, or the app is small enough that
broad re-renders don’t matter.

• You want zero extra dependencies.
Use Redux (or a similar library) when:

• The state changes frequently and many different, unrelated parts of the UI need to read slices of it.

• You need middleware for async flows, or you want time-travel debugging to make sense of complex state changes.

• The update logic itself is complex enough that you want it centralized and testable (pure reducers) rather than scattered across components.
A practical note: most modern apps don’t reach for Redux for everything.
A common, sane split is: local component state for things only one component cares about, Context for slow-changing global data (theme/auth/locale), and Redux — or a lighter alternative like Zustand or Jotai, which give Redux-style fine-grained subscriptions with far less boilerplate — for state that’s both shared widely and changes often. The deciding question isn’t “Context or Redux” in the abstract, it’s “how often does this change, and how many components need to react precisely to that change?”
